analogy: Think of "The Origin and Evolution of the Earth" through the chapter's own opening images: FUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 12 THE EARTH This unit deals with • Origin and evolution of the earth; Interior of the earth; Wegener’s continental drift theory and plate tectonics; earthquakes and volcanoes UNIT II Do you remember the nursery rhyme “…Twinkle, twinkle little star…”? Starry... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: THE EARTH, EARLY THEORIES, THE ORIGIN AND EVOLUTION, OF THE EARTH. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
The Origin and Evolution of the Earth is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 12 THE EARTH This unit deals with • Origin and evolution of the earth; Interior of the earth; Wegener’s continental drift theory and plate tectonics; earthquakes and volcanoes UNIT II

The text explains that do you remember the nursery rhyme “…Twinkle, twinkle little star…”?

The text explains that starry nights have always attracted us since the childhood.

The text explains that you may also have thought of these stars and had numerous questions in your mind.

The text explains that questions such as how many stars are there in the sky?

The text explains that may be many more such questions are still there in your mind.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will learn how these “twinkling little stars” were formed.

The text explains that with that you will eventually also read the story of origin and evolution of the earth.

The text explains that eARLY THEORIES Origin of the Earth A large number of hypotheses were put forth by different philosophers and scientists regarding the origin of the earth.

The text explains that one of the earlier and popular arguments was by German philosopher Immanuel Kant.

The text explains that the hypothesis considered that the planets were formed out of a cloud of material associated with a youthful sun, which was slowly rotating.

The text explains that in 1950, Otto Schmidt in Russia and Carl Weizascar in Germany somewhat revised the ‘nebular hypothesis’, though differing in details.

The text explains that they considered that the sun was surrounded by solar nebula containing mostly the hydrogen and helium along with what may be termed as dust.

The text explains that the friction and collision of particles led to formation of a disk-shaped cloud and the planets were THE ORIGIN AND EVOLUTION OF THE EARTH C H A P T E R formed through the process of accretion.

The text explains that however, scientists in later period took up the problems of origin of universe rather than that of just the earth or the planets.

The text explains that mODERN THEORIES Origin of the Universe The most popular argument regarding the origin of the universe is the Big Bang Theory.

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 14 Figure 2.1 : The Big Bang unimaginably small volume, infinite temperature and infinite density.

The text explains that (iii) Within 300,000 years from the Big Bang, temperature dropped to 4,500 K (Kelvin) and gave rise to atomic matter.

The text explains that these initial density differences gave rise to differences in gravitational forces and it caused the matter to get drawn together.

The text explains that the mean distance between the sun and the earth is 149,598,000 km.

The text explains that the gravitational force within the lumps leads to the formation of a core to the gas cloud and a huge rotating disc of gas and dust develops around the gas core.

The text explains that tHE ORIGIN AND EVOLUTION OF THE EARTH 15 (ii) In the next stage, the gas cloud starts getting condensed and the matter around the core develops into small- rounded objects.

The text explains that these small-rounded objects by the process of cohesion develop into what is called planetesimals.

The text explains that e E E E EVOLUTION VOLUTION VOLUTION VOLUTION VOLUTION OF OF OF OF OF THE THE THE THE THE E E E E EARTH ARTH ARTH ARTH ARTH Do you know that the planet earth initially was a barren, rocky and hot object with a thin atmosphere of hydrogen and helium.

The text explains that this is far from the present day picture of the earth.

The text explains that hence, there must have been some events– processes, which may have caused this change from rocky, barren and hot earth to a beautiful planet with ample amount of water and conducive atmosphere favouring the existence of life.

The text explains that from the outermost end of the atmosphere to the centre of the earth, the material that exists is not uniform.

The text explains that the atmospheric matter has the least density.

The text explains that from the surface to deeper depths, the earth’s interior has different zones and each of these contains materials with different characteristics.

The text explains that how was the layered structure of the earth developed?

The text explains that evolution of Lithosphere Evolution of Lithosphere Evolution of Lithosphere Evolution of Lithosphere Evolution of Lithosphere The earth was mostly in a volatile state during its primordial stage.

The text explains that due to gradual increase in density the temperature inside has increased.

The text explains that this allowed heavier materials (like iron) to sink towards the centre of the earth and the lighter ones to move towards the surface.

The text explains that this later led to the development of the outer surface in the form of a crust.

The text explains that during the formation of the moon, due to the giant impact, the earth was further heated up.

The text explains that it is through the process of differentiation that the earth forming material got separated into different layers.

The text explains that starting from the surface to the central parts, we have layers like the crust, mantle, outer core and inner core.

The text explains that from the crust to the core, the density of the material increases.

The text explains that we shall discuss in detail the properties of each of this layer in the next chapter.

The text explains that evolution of Atmosphere and Hydrosphere Evolution of Atmosphere and Hydrosphere Evolution of Atmosphere and Hydrosphere Evolution of Atmosphere and Hydrosphere Evolution of Atmosphere and Hydrosphere The present composition of earth’s atmosphere is chiefly contributed by nitrogen and oxygen.

The text explains that you will be dealing with the composition and structure of the earth’s atmosphere in Chapter 8.

The text explains that in the second stage, the hot interior of the earth contributed to the evolution of the atmosphere.

The text explains that finally, the composition of the atmosphere was modified by the living world through the process of photosynthesis.

The text explains that (a) Solar winds (c) Degassing (b) Differentiation (d) Photosynthesis (iii) Life on the earth appeared around how many years before the present?

The text explains that (a) 13.7 billion (c) 4.6 billion (b) 3.8 million (d) 3.8 billion 2.

The text explains that (i) What is meant by the process of differentiation?

The text explains that (ii) What was the nature of the earth surface initially?

The text explains that (iii) What were the gases which initially formed the earth’s atmosphere?

The text explains that (i) Write an explanatory note on the ‘Big Bang Theory’.

The text explains that (ii) List the stages in the evolution of the earth and explain each stage in brief.

The text explains that (i) Which is the agency that has launched this project?

The text explains that (ii) Why are scientists interested in collecting Stardust?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 2: The Origin and Evolution of the Earth

prelims_traps: Do not treat THE EARTH and EARLY THEORIES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Origin and Evolution of the Earth; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Origin and Evolution of the Earth through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use THE EARTH and EARLY THEORIES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
